name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَٱتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ ءَايَـٰتِنَا فَٱنسَلَخَ مِنْهَا فَأَتْبَعَهُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ فَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْغَاوِينَ (١٧٥)
```

```english
Relate to them an account of him to whom We gave Our signs, but he cast them off. Thereupon Satan pursued him, and he became one of the perverse. (175)
```

```arabic
وَلَوْ شِئْنَا لَرَفَعْنَـٰهُ بِهَا وَلَـٰكِنَّهُۥٓ أَخْلَدَ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱتَّبَعَ هَوَىٰهُ ۚ فَمَثَلُهُۥ كَمَثَلِ ٱلْكَلْبِ إِن تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْهِ يَلْهَثْ أَوْ تَتْرُكْهُ يَلْهَث ۚ ذَّٰلِكَ مَثَلُ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا ۚ فَٱقْصُصِ ٱلْقَصَصَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (١٧٦)
```

```english
Had We wished, We would have surely raised him by their means, but he clung to the earth and followed his [base] desires. So his parable is that of a dog: if you make for it, it lolls out its tongue, and if you let it alone, it lolls out its tongue. Such is the parable of the people who deny Our signs. So recount these narratives, so that they may reflect. (176)
```

```arabic
سَآءَ مَثَلًا ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَأَنفُسَهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَظْلِمُونَ (١٧٧)
```

```english
Evil is the parable of the people who deny Our signs and wrong themselves. (177)
```

```arabic
مَن يَهْدِ ٱللَّهُ فَهُوَ ٱلْمُهْتَدِى ۖ وَمَن يُضْلِلْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (١٧٨)
```

```english
Whomever Allah guides is rightly guided, and whomever He leads astray—it is they who are the losers. (178)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ ذَرَأْنَا لِجَهَنَّمَ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ ۖ لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ لَّا يَفْقَهُونَ بِهَا وَلَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ لَّا يُبْصِرُونَ بِهَا وَلَهُمْ ءَاذَانٌ لَّا يَسْمَعُونَ بِهَآ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ كَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْغَـٰفِلُونَ (١٧٩)
```

```english
Certainly We have winnowed out for hell many of the jinn and humans: they have hearts with which they do not understand, they have eyes with which they do not see, they have ears with which they do not hear. They are like cattle; indeed, they are more astray. It is they who are the heedless. (179)
```

## (Commentary)

This is another story from the chronicles of the Children of Israel, namely the report of Bal‘am bin Bā‘ūrā’. Allah commanded His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to recite it to them so it might become clear that mere connection to ordinary, outward causes is insufficient for human success or the guarantee of felicity unless Allah so wills. Furthermore, Allah does not will such success for one who clings to the earth and follows his own desires, for his destination is the Fire. He then mentions the sign (آية) of this condition within them: they do not employ their hearts, eyes, or ears for what benefits them; the comprehensive sign is that they are heedless (غافلون).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ الَّذِي آتَيْناهُ آياتِنا فَانْسَلَخَ مِنْها** — **"Relate to them the story of him to whom We gave Our signs, but he cast them off"** (7:175), to the end of the verse. The meaning of "giving the signs," as suggested by the context, is being invested with inner signs (الآيات الأنفسية) and specific internal charismata (الكرامات الخاصة الباطنية) by which the path of the knowledge of Allah (معرفة الله) is illuminated for him, and things are disclosed to him such that no doubt regarding the Truth remains. "Casting off" (الانسلاخ) refers to a thing exiting or being stripped from its skin; it is a metaphorical allusion (كناية استعارية) to the fact that the signs were as inseparable from him as skin is to a body, yet the filth within his essence caused him to emerge from them. "Following" (الإتباع) is like *al-taba‘* (التبع); *al-ittibā‘* (الإتباع) means pursuing and tracing the footsteps or tracks. It is said: *taba‘a*, *atba‘a*, and *ittaba‘a*, all carrying the same meaning. "Perversity" (الغي) and "waywardness" (الغواية) refer to deviation (الضلال), as if it were an exit from the path due to an inability to preserve the objective (المقصد) to which the path leads; thus, it involves forgetting the objective and the goal. One who is bewildered in his affair while on the path is "perverted" (غوي), whereas one who exits the path while remembering his objective is "astray" (ضال). This latter sense is more appropriate for the context of the verse, for the subject of the report, after casting off the signs of Allah and being pursued by Satan, lost sight of the path of rectitude (سبيل الرشد) and was unable to save himself from the abyss of destruction. Sometimes, both waywardness and deviation are used synonymously to mean exiting the path that leads to the goal.

The commentators have differed regarding the identity of the subject of this report in this verse, offering various opinions. We shall point to most or all of them in the forthcoming "Discussion on Traditions," Allah willing.

The verse—as you see—leaves his name ambiguous and restricts itself to an overview of his story. Nevertheless, it is apparent that it is an actual report (نبأ واقع) and not merely a parable (تمثيل); thus, there is no weight to the claim of those who say it is a mere parable without an actual historical event.

The meaning is: "And recite to them"—meaning to the Children of Israel or to people in general—news of a momentous matter, which is the "report" (نبأ) of the man "to whom We gave Our signs" (الَّذِي آتَيْناهُ آياتِنا), disclosing to his inner self great divine marks and traces by which the truth of the matter was illuminated for him, "but he cast them off" (فَانْسَلَخَ مِنْها) and rejected them after they had been inseparable from him, "so Satan pursued him, and he became one of the perverse" (فَأَتْبَعَهُ الشَّيْطانُ فَكانَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ), unable to save himself from destruction.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَوْ شِئْنا لَرَفَعْناهُ بِها وَلكِنَّهُ أَخْلَدَ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ وَاتَّبَعَ هَواهُ** — **"Had We wished, We would have surely raised him by their means, but he clung to the earth and followed his passions"** (7:176). "Clinging" (الإخلاد) means permanent adherence. "Clinging to the earth" (الإخلاد إلى الأرض) means sticking to it, which is a metaphor (كناية) for the inclination toward enjoying worldly pleasures and committing oneself to them. "Panting" (اللهاث) in a dog refers to it lolling its tongue out due to thirst.

**So His saying:** "Had We wished, We would have surely raised him by their means" (وَلَوْ شِئْنا لَرَفَعْناهُ بِها) means: had We willed, We would have elevated him through those signs and brought him close to Us, for in proximity to Allah is an elevation from the depths of this world—which, by occupying man with itself away from Allah and His signs, is the lowest of the low. His being raised by those signs is because they are outward divine causes (أسباب إلهية ظاهرية) that facilitate the guidance of one who is invested with them. However, they do not necessitate felicity for man, because the completion of their effect in that regard is contingent upon the will of Allah. Allah, the Glorified, does not will this for one who turns away from Him and turns toward something else—namely, the earthly life that distracts from Allah and the Abode of His Grace. Indeed, turning away from Allah, the Glorified, and denying His signs is injustice (ظلم), and the word has been justly passed from Him, the Glorified, that He does not guide the unjust folk, and that those who disbelieve and deny Our signs are the inmates of the Fire, abiding therein forever.

For this reason, He, the Exalted, followed His saying, "Had We wished, We would have surely raised him by their means," with His saying, "but he clung to the earth and followed his passions." The implied meaning is: but We did not will that, because he clung to the earth and followed his desires, and that became an occasion for Our leading him astray, not for Our guidance, as He, the Exalted, said: "Allah leads the wrongdoers astray, and Allah does whatever He wishes" (14:27).

And His saying: **فَمَثَلُهُ كَمَثَلِ الْكَلْبِ إِنْ تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْهِ يَلْهَثْ أَوْ تَتْرُكْهُ يَلْهَثْ** — **"So his parable is that of a dog: if you make an onset on him, he pants, and if you leave him alone, he pants"** (7:176). This means he possesses this innate disposition (سجية) and does not abandon it, whether you rebuke and restrain him or leave him be. "Make an onset" (تَحْمِلْ) is from *al-hamlah* (the charge/onset), not from *al-haml* (carrying a burden). **ذلِكَ مَثَلُ الْقَوْمِ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا** — **"That is the parable of the people who deny Our signs"** (7:176). Thus, denial (التكذيب) is an innate disposition and a wicked, inseparable psychic state (هيئة نفسانية) for them; Our signs continue to be repeated to their senses, and the denial of them continues to be repeated by them. **فَاقْصُصِ الْقَصَصَ** — **"So relate [these] stories"** (7:176). *Al-qasas* is a verbal noun (مصدر), meaning "relate stories," or a noun (اسم مصدر), meaning "relate the story." **لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ** — **"so that they may reflect"** (7:176), and thereby submit to the Truth and extract themselves from falsehood.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ساءَ مَثَلاً الْقَوْمُ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا وَأَنْفُسَهُمْ كانُوا يَظْلِمُونَ** — **"Evil is the parable of the people who deny Our signs and used to wrong themselves"** (7:177). This is a condemnation of them by way of their description, and an announcement to them that they do not harm anything by denying His signs; rather, it is an injustice they commit against themselves, as no one else is harmed by it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مَنْ يَهْدِ اللهُ فَهُوَ الْمُهْتَدِي وَمَنْ يُضْلِلْ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْخاسِرُونَ** — **"Whomever Allah guides, he is the rightly guided, and whomever He leads astray—it is they who are the losers"** (7:178). The definite article (اللام) in "the guided" (الْمُهْتَدِي) and "the losers" (الْخاسِرُونَ) signifies perfection (الكمال) rather than exclusivity (الحصر), apparently. The import of the verse is that mere guidance toward something does not benefit at all, nor does it produce the effect of guidance, unless it is accompanied by the guidance of Allah (هداية الله), the Glorified; for it is through His guidance that guidance is perfected and felicity is guaranteed. Similarly, mere error does not cause definitive harm except by the addition of Allah's leading astray (إضلال الله) to it; at that point, its effect is completed and loss (الخسران) becomes inevitable.

Thus, the mere connection of individuals to the causes of felicity—such as the outward form of faith and godwariness (تقوى)—and their being invested with them does not bring them to the station of salvation. Likewise, their connection to and investment with the causes of error does not bring them to the station of destruction and loss, unless Allah so wills. Thus, by His will, He guides whom He guides and leads astray whom He leads astray.

The meaning, therefore, resolves to the fact that guidance is only true guidance (هداية حقيقية) upon which its effects follow if Allah's will is present within it; otherwise, it is a mere form of guidance and not its reality. The same applies to leading astray. If you wish, you could say: the discourse indicates the restriction of true guidance to Allah, the Glorified, as well as leading astray—and He leads none astray thereby except the transgressors (الفاسقين).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ ذَرَأْنا لِجَهَنَّمَ كَثِيراً مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ** — **"Certainly We have wonnowed for hell many of the jinn and humans"** (7:179), to the end of the verse. "Winnowing/Creating" (الذرء) is creation (الخلق). Allah, the Glorified, has identified Hell as the end-goal (غاية) for the creation of many jinn and humans. This does not contradict what is identified elsewhere—that the goal for the creation of the creation is mercy (الرحمة), which is Paradise in the Hereafter—as in His saying: "except those on whom your Lord has mercy—and for that He created them" (11:119). For the "purpose" (الغرض) differs in meaning according to the perfection of the act (كمال الفعل) and the ultimate end (نهاية الفعل) at which the act terminates.

To explain this: when a carpenter intends to make a door, he turns to pieces of wood he has prepared for it, designs them, and then begins sawing, carving, and turning until he completes the door. The perfection of his purpose (كمال غرضه) in performing the action upon those pieces of wood is the attainment of the door and nothing else; this is from one perspective. From another perspective, he knows from the very beginning that not all parts of those pieces of wood are suitable to be parts of the door, for the door has a specific form that is incompatible with the [original] form of the wood. In changing their form, some parts must inevitably be wasted as they fall outside the design of the work. The becoming of these portions as waste (فضلة) to be discarded is included in the intention of the maker; it is willed by him with a volition called "necessary intent" (قصدا ضروريا). Thus, the carpenter, in making the door relative to the wood before him, has two types of goals: first, the "perfective goal" (الغاية الكمالية), which is to make a door from it; and second, the "subsidiary goal" (الغاية التابعة), which is to make some of it into a door and make some of it into waste that is of no use and is discarded, due to its lack of readiness (استعداد) to take on the form of the door.

Similarly, a farmer sows a field to harvest wheat, yet only some of the seed he spent remains pure until the day of harvest; the rest goes to waste, lost in the earth, spoiled by pests, or trampled by livestock. All of this is intended by the farmer from one perspective, while the harvested wheat is intended from another.

The Divine Will (المشية الإلهية) has attached itself to creating from the earth a sound human being who worships Him and thereby enters His mercy. However, the variation in acquired predispositions (الاستعدادات المكتسبة) from worldly life—with its various influences—does not allow every individual of this species to run in his true course and follow the path of salvation, except for those granted success (توفيق). At that point, the goals differ. It is correct to say that Allah, the Glorified, has a goal in the creation of man, for instance, which is to encompass them with His mercy and enter them into His Paradise; and it is also correct to say that Allah has a goal regarding the people of loss and wretchedness of this species, which is to enter them into the Fire, even though He had created them for Paradise. However, the first goal is the "original, perfective goal" (غاية أصلية كمالية), while the second goal is a "subsidiary, necessary goal" (غاية تبعية ضرورية). The Divine Decree (القضاء الإلهي) concerning the felicity of the felicitous and the wretchedness of the wretched looks toward this second type of goal, for He, the Exalted, knows what the state of the creation will resolve to in terms of felicity or wretchedness; thus, He wills that with a subsidiary, not an original, volition.

It is upon this type of goal that His saying, the Exalted, is revealed: "Certainly We have wonnowed for hell many of the jinn and humans," as well as other verses in this vein, which are many.

His saying: **لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ لا يَفْقَهُونَ بِها وَلَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ لا يُبْصِرُونَ بِها وَلَهُمْ آذانٌ لا يَسْمَعُونَ بِها** — **"They have hearts with which they do not understand, they have eyes with which they do not see, and they have ears with which they do not hear"** (7:179), is an allusion to the nullification of their readiness (استعداد) to fall into the course of Divine Mercy and to stand in the path of Lordly breezes. Thus, they are not benefited by the signs of Allah they witness, the exhortations of the people of truth they hear, or the proof and evidence their primordial nature (فطرة) dictates to them.

Neither the intellect, the eye, nor the ear becomes corrupted in its function, for Allah created them for that, and He has said: "There is no altering Allah’s creation" (30:30)—unless the one who changes it is Allah, the Glorified, in which case it would be part of creation. However, He, the Glorified, does not change what He has bestowed upon a people until they change what is within themselves. He, the Exalted, said: "That is because Allah never changes a blessing that He has bestowed on a people unless they change what is in their own souls" (8:53).

So, the one who nullified the readiness they possessed and corrupted the functions of their hearts, eyes, and ears is Allah, the Glorified; He did to them what He did as a recompense for what they earned, as an exemplary punishment (نكالا). They changed the blessing of Allah by changing the path of servitude (العبودية), so Allah recompensed them by sealing (الطبع) their hearts so they do not understand, placing a veil (الغشاوة) over their eyes so they do not see, and a heaviness (الوقر) in their ears so they do not hear. This is the sign (آية) that they are being driven toward the Fire.

His saying: **أُولئِكَ كَالْأَنْعامِ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ** — **"They are like cattle; rather they are more astray"** (7:179), is the result of what preceded and an explanation of their state. They have lost that which distinguishes man from other animals: the discernment of good and evil, and the beneficial and the harmful, relative to a felicitous human life, through the channels of hearing, sight, and the heart.

They were specifically likened to cattle (الأنعام) among the mute animals—even though they possess the traits and characteristics of predatory beasts as well as those of grazing cattle—because enjoyment through eating and mating is more primary and prior relative to the animal nature (الطبع الحيواني). Seeking benefit is prior to repelling harm. The defensive, irascible powers (القوى الدافعة الغضبية) in man are intended for the sake of the attractive, appetitive powers (القوى الجاذبة الشهوية) within him. The goal of the species, according to its animal life, pertains primarily to nutrition and reproduction, and it preserves this by employing the defensive powers. Thus, the verse follows the course of His saying, the Exalted: "As for the faithless, they enjoy and eat just as the cattle eat, and the Fire will be their abode" (47:12).

As for their being "more astray" (أشد ضلالا) than cattle—which implies the existence of some "astray-ness" in cattle—it is because the deviation in cattle is relative (نسبي), not real (حقيقي). Cattle are guided according to the composite powers they possess that urge them to restrict their concern to eating and enjoyment; they are not astray regarding the felicity of life for which they were prepared, nor are they deserving of blame for the course they have taken. They are only considered "astray" when measured against human felicity, which they do not possess and for which they were not equipped with the means to attain.

As for these individuals whose hearts, eyes, and ears have been sealed, the felicity is *their* felicity, and they were equipped with the hearing, sight, and hearts that lead them to it and point them toward it. However, they corrupted these faculties, wasted their functions, and relegated them to the status of the hearing, sight, and hearts found in cattle, using them for what cattle use them for: the enjoyment of the pleasures of the belly and the private parts. Thus, they are more astray than cattle, and to them the blame returns.

His saying: **أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْغافِلُونَ** — **"It is they who are the heedless"** (7:179), is a conclusion and an explanation of another state of theirs: that the reality of heedlessness (الغفلة) is what is found in them. For it is by the will of Allah, the Glorified, that He clothed them with it through the seal (الطبع) He placed upon their hearts, eyes, and ears. Heedlessness is the substance of every deviation and falsehood.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَاتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ الَّذِي آتَيْناهُ آياتِنا** (Relate to them the story of him to whom We gave Our signs), it is said: My father narrated to me from al-Husayn bin Khalid, from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (عليه‌السلام): **أنه أعطي بلعم بن باعوراء الاسم الأعظم ، وكان يدعو به فيستجيب له فمال إلى فرعون ـ فلما مر فرعون في طلب موسى وأصحابه ـ قال فرعون لبلعم : ادع الله على موسى وأصحابه ليحبسه علينا ـ فركب حمارته ليمر في طلب موسى ـ فامتنعت عليه حمارته فأقبل يضربها ـ فأنطقها الله عز وجل فقالت ويلك على ما ذا تضربني؟ أتريد أن أجيء معك لتدعو على نبي الله وقوم مؤمنين؟ ولم يزل يضربها حتى قتلها فانسلخ الاسم من لسانه ، وهو قوله : « فَانْسَلَخَ مِنْها فَأَتْبَعَهُ الشَّيْطانُ فَكانَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ ، وَلَوْ شِئْنا لَرَفَعْناهُ بِها ـ وَلكِنَّهُ أَخْلَدَ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ وَاتَّبَعَ هَواهُ ـ فَمَثَلُهُ كَمَثَلِ الْكَلْبِ إِنْ تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْهِ يَلْهَثْ أَوْ تَتْرُكْهُ يَلْهَثْ » وهو مثل ضربه الله.** — "Bal‘am bin Bā‘ūrā’ was given the Greatest Name (الاسم الأعظم), and he used to supplicate with it and be answered. He inclined toward Pharaoh. When Pharaoh went out in pursuit of Moses and his companions, Pharaoh said to Bal‘am: 'Supplicate to Allah against Moses and his companions so that He may stall them for us.' So he mounted his she-ass to go in pursuit of Moses, but his she-ass refused him. He began to beat her, so Allah (عز وجل) made her speak, and she said: 'Woe to you! For what do you beat me? Do you want me to go with you so you can supplicate against a Prophet of Allah and a believing people?' He continued to beat her until he killed her. Then the Name was stripped from his tongue, and that is His saying: 'but he cast them off, so Satan pursued him, and he became one of the perverse. Had We wished, We would have surely raised him by their means, but he clung to the earth and followed his passions... so his parable is that of a dog: if you make an onset on him, he pants, and if you leave him alone, he pants.' This is a parable that Allah has struck."

**I say:** His saying (عليه‌السلام), "This is a parable that Allah has struck," apparently refers to the report of Bal‘am. The discussion regarding the meaning of the Greatest Name (الاسم الأعظم) will come in the commentary on the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى), Allah willing.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Faryabi, ‘Abd al-Razzaq, ‘Abd bin Humayd, al-Nasa’i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from ‘Abdullah bin Mas‘ud regarding His saying: **وَاتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ الَّذِي آتَيْناهُ آياتِنا فَانْسَلَخَ مِنْها** — "He was a man from the Children of Israel called Bal‘am bin Abar."

In the same source, ‘Abd bin Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated through various chains from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "He is Bal‘am bin Bā‘ūrā’"—and in another wording: "Bal‘am bin ‘Amir"—"who was given the Name and was among the Children of Israel."

**I say:** The fact that his name was Bal‘am and that he was from the Children of Israel has been narrated from others besides Ibn ‘Abbas, and other things have also been narrated from him.

In *Ruh al-Ma‘ani*, mentioning the opinion that the verse was revealed concerning Umayyah bin Abi al-Salt al-Thaqafi, the poet: "He had read the ancient books and knew that Allah (تعالى) would send a messenger, so he hoped that he would be that messenger. It happened that he went to Bahrain, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was commissioned as a prophet. He stayed there for eight years and then returned. He met the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) among a group of his companions, who invited him to Islam and recited Surah Ya-Sin to him. When he finished, Umayyah stood up, dragging his feet. Quraysh followed him, saying: 'What do you say, O Umayyah?' He said: 'Until I look into his affair.'

He went to Syria and returned after the Battle of Badr intending to submit to Islam. When he was informed of the battle, he abandoned Islam and said: 'If he were a prophet, he would not have killed his kinsmen.' So he went to Ta'if and died there.

His sister, al-Fari‘ah, came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and he asked her about his death. She mentioned to him that he recited at his death:

*Every life, even if it lasts an age,*
*Is destined one day to vanish.*
*Would that I had been, before what appeared to me,*
*In the mountain peaks, tending the mountain goats.*
*Indeed, the Day of Reckoning is a momentous day,*
*In which the small child turns gray—a heavy day.*

Then he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to her: 'Recite to me some of your brother's poetry.' So she recited:

*To You belong praise, blessings, and grace, our Lord,*
*And nothing is higher than You in majesty or glory.*
*A King upon the throne of heaven, a Guardian,*
*To whose might faces humble themselves and prostrate.*

From a long poem until she reached its end.

Then she recited to him his poem in which he says:

*The people stood for the reckoning together,*
*The wretched punished, and the felicitous.*

And in which is:

*Before the Possessor of the Throne they are presented,*
*He knows the manifest and the hidden secret.*
*The day the All-Beneficent comes, and He is Merciful,*
*Indeed, His promise was destined to be fulfilled.*
*Lord, if You forgive, then forgiveness is my hope,*
*Or if You punish, You do not punish an innocent.*

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Your brother's poetry believed, but his heart disbelieved'—and Allah (تعالى) revealed the verse."

**I say:** The story is a compilation from several narrations. In *al-Majma‘*, the summary of the story is mentioned, and it is noted that the revelation of the verse concerning him is narrated from ‘Abdullah bin ‘Umar, Sa‘id bin al-Musayyib, Zayd bin Aslam, and Abu Rawq. However, it is apparent that the verses are Meccan, having been revealed with the Surah in Mecca, and what they mentioned is by way of application (التطبيق).

In *al-Majma‘*: "It is said: He is Abu ‘Amir bin al-Nu‘man bin Sayfi, the monk—whom the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) called 'the Transgressor' (الفاسق). He had practiced monkery in the pre-Islamic period and wore haircloth. He came to Medina and said to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): 'What is this that you have brought?' He said: 'I have brought the primordial monotheism (الحنيفية), the religion of Abraham.' He said: 'I am upon it.' The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'You are not upon it; rather, you have introduced into it what is not of it.' Abu ‘Amir said: 'May Allah cause the liar among us to die alone and an outcast.'

He went to the people of Syria and sent word to the hypocrites to prepare weapons. Then he went to Caesar and brought an army to expel the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from Medina. He died in Syria, alone and an outcast." Narrated from Sa‘id bin al-Musayyib.

**I say:** The objection that the Surah is Meccan is valid, and many stories have been narrated in this regard that are of no use to exhaustively list.

In the same source, Abu Ja‘far (عليه‌السلام) said: **الأصل في ذلك بلعم ثم ضربه الله مثلا ـ لكل مؤثر هواه على هدى الله من أهل القبلة.** — "The origin in this is Bal‘am, then Allah struck him as a parable for everyone among the people of the Qiblah who prefers his passion over the guidance of Allah."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (عليه‌السلام) regarding His saying: **لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ لا يَفْقَهُونَ بِها** — "He says: Allah sealed them, so they do not reason. **وَلَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ** — 'and they have eyes' upon which is a covering from guidance, **لا يُبْصِرُونَ بِها ـ وَلَهُمْ آذانٌ لا يَسْمَعُونَ بِها** — 'they do not see with them—and they have ears with which they do not hear,' meaning He placed a heaviness in their ears, so they will never hear the guidance."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Bayhaqi narrated in *al-Asma’ wa al-Sifat* from ‘Abdullah bin ‘Amr bin al-‘As, who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وسلم) saying: **إن الله خلق خلقه في ظلمة ثم ألقى عليهم من نوره ـ فمن أصابه من ذلك النور يومئذ شيء اهتدى. ومن أخطأ ضل.** — 'Indeed, Allah created His creation in darkness, then He cast upon them some of His light. Whoever was touched by some of that light on that day was guided, and whoever was missed by it went astray.'"

In the same source, al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Abi al-Dunya in *Maka’id al-Shaytan*, Abu Ya‘la, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu al-Darda’, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: **خلق الله الجن ثلاثة أصناف : صنف حيات وعقارب وخشاش الأرض ، وصنف كالريح في الهواء ، وصنف عليهم الحساب والعقاب ، وخلق الله الإنس ثلاثة أصناف : صنف كالبهائم قال الله : « لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ لا يَفْقَهُونَ بِها ، وَلَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ لا يُبْصِرُونَ بِها ـ وَلَهُمْ آذانٌ لا يَسْمَعُونَ بِها ـ أُولئِكَ كَالْأَنْعامِ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ » وجنس أجسادهم أجساد بني آدم ـ وأرواحهم أرواح الشياطين ، وصنف في ظل الله يوم لا ظل إلا ظله.** — "Allah created the jinn in three categories: one category of snakes, scorpions, and creeping things of the earth; one category like the wind in the air; and one category upon whom is the reckoning and the punishment. And Allah created humans in three categories: one category like cattle, of whom Allah said: 'They have hearts with which they do not understand, they have eyes with which they do not see, and they have ears with which they do not hear—they are like cattle; rather they are more astray'; a class whose bodies are the bodies of the children of Adam but whose spirits are the spirits of satans; and a category in the shade of Allah on the day when there is no shade but His shade."

**I say:** The discussion regarding the jinn and the satans among humans will come in an appropriate place, Allah (تعالى) willing.
